flashcards.Back: A feature within Cisco DNA Center that uses AI and machine learning to identify, profile, and monitor endpoints on the network with high precision.
The ability of AI Endpoint Analytics to discover and track every device connected to the network, including IoT, medical, and industrial equipment.
The process of collecting data about a device (like OS, model, and hardware) to automatically categorize it into a specific device type or group.
Using advanced mathematical models to recognize devices based on their behavioral patterns rather than just simple MAC addresses or hostnames.
A technique used by AI Endpoint Analytics to examine the data portion of packets to accurately identify application protocols and device types.
An AI-powered security feature that detects if a malicious device is attempting to impersonate a legitimate endpoint by mimicking its hardware profile.
The automated sorting of discovered devices into structured categories like 'Workstation,' 'IP Camera,' or 'Smart Sensor' for policy enforcement.
Learning the 'normal' traffic patterns of a device so that deviations can be detected as potential security threats or hardware malfunctions.
A key source of telemetry data for AI Endpoint Analytics, providing details on flow volume, duration, and protocol usage for every device.
Analyzing the domain name requests made by an endpoint to help identify its manufacturer, service type, and intended function.
Examining the User-Agent string in web traffic to determine the operating system and browser version used by a connected device.
A security metric assigned to an endpoint based on its identity verification and behavioral consistency over time.
The specialized handling of Internet of Things devices that often lack traditional management agents and have unique communication profiles.
The ability for administrators to create unique identification rules for proprietary hardware that is not already in the Cisco AI database.
The connection between DNA Center and ISE where endpoint profile data is used to automatically apply security access policies.
A connected endpoint that AI Analytics has discovered but cannot yet identify. These often require manual review or more data for classification.
The capability to distinguish between very similar devices, such as different models of IP phones from the same manufacturer.
A centralized view in DNA Center that shows the status, location, and type of every endpoint currently on the network.
The use of anonymized data from thousands of Cisco customers to improve the device identification accuracy for everyone.
The continuous monitoring of specialized protocols like DICOM (medical) or Modbus (industrial) to identify niche equipment.
Linking specific technical data points (like MAC OUI or TCP ports) to human-readable device labels in the analytics engine.
The secure link where DNA Center sends telemetry data to Cisco's cloud AI for advanced analysis and model updates.
Using the identified device type to automatically place the endpoint into the correct virtual network or security group.
The automatic alerting when a device starts communicating in a way that is inconsistent with its assigned profile (e.g., a printer sending mass emails).
The first 24 bits of a MAC address used as a basic attribute to identify the hardware manufacturer of an endpoint.
Analyzing the options and sequence of a device's DHCP request to help determine its operating system and device class.
Monitoring discovery protocols to find devices like Apple TVs or printers that announce their services on the local subnet.
Gathering device information by simply observing network traffic, without sending any disruptive probes to the endpoint.
Sending specific requests (like SNMP or HTTP) to an endpoint to elicit a response that helps confirm its identity and capabilities.
The speed at which the AI engine can gather enough data to move a device from 'unknown' to 'identified'.
